title: Sri Lankan Parippu (Coconut Dhal)

ingredients.name: masoor dal (red lentils)
visual_checks: Rinse until the water runs clear.
ingredients.notes: Rinse until the water runs clear.

subs.name: Toor dal or split moong dal work well — the texture becomes slightly thicker and earthier but the dish remains equally delicious.

ingredients.name: coconut milk, full-fat
visual_checks: Added after the lentils are cooked to keep the coconut flavour fresh.
ingredients.notes: Added after the lentils are cooked to keep the coconut flavour fresh.

subs.name: 50 g coconut milk powder dissolved in 200 ml water; alternatively omit for a plain tempered dal that is lighter and less rich.
Cumin seeds in equal quantity for the tempering give a warmer, less sharp flavour that blends beautifully with the coconut.

ingredients.name: dried red chilli, broken in half
cooking oil
visual_checks: Coconut oil gives the most authentic aroma.
ingredients.notes: Coconut oil gives the most authentic aroma.

steps.text: Place the rinsed masoor dal in a heavy-bottomed pot with 600 ml water and the turmeric. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, skimming off any foam that rises. Reduce heat and simmer uncovered until the lentils are completely soft and breaking apart — about 15 minutes.

Pour in the coconut milk and stir well to combine. Add the red chilli powder and coriander powder. Cook on low heat for 5 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the dal thickens to a pourable consistency. Season with salt and keep on the lowest flame.

Heat oil in a small frying pan over medium heat. Add the mustard seeds and wait for them to crackle and pop — about 30 seconds. Add the curry leaves and broken dried red chilli; they will spit briefly in the hot oil.

Add the sliced onion and fry, stirring, until it turns golden brown — about 5 minutes. Stir in the garlic, green chillies, and cumin seeds. Cook for 1 more minute until the garlic is lightly golden and the raw smell is gone.
Pour the entire hot tempering over the coconut dhal and stir it in. Taste and adjust salt. Serve immediately alongside steamed rice, hoppers, or roti — the tempering aroma is best while still fresh.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: masoor-dal → Toor dal or split moong dal work well — the texture becomes slightly thicker and earthier but the dish remains equally delicious.; coconut-milk → 50 g coconut milk powder dissolved in 200 ml water; alternatively omit for a plain tempered dal that is lighter and less rich.; mustard-seeds → Cumin seeds in equal quantity for the tempering give a warmer, less sharp flavour that blends beautifully with the coconut.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Pressure-cook the masoor dal with turmeric and water for 2 whistles on medium flame, then open and proceed from step 2. The tempering technique is nearly identical to South Indian tadka dal — the only difference is the coconut milk finish, which is widely available in Indian grocery stores. Coconut oil for the tempering is optional but worth using if you have it.
